title: Quantized Conductance through Surface States in High Quality Three-Dimensional Dirac Semimetal Cd$_3$As$_2$ Nanowire/Nanoribbon p-n Junctions
authors: Sungjin An, Zhuo Bin Siu, Vardan Kaladzhyan, Jens H. Bardarson, Sunghun Lee, Myoung-Jae Lee, Kidong Park, Jeunghee Park, Mansoor B. A. Jalil, Jungpil Seo, Minkyung Jung
abstract: We report the observation of quantized conductance in high-mobility three-dimensional Dirac semimetal Cd$_3$As$_2$ nanowire and nanoribbon p-n junctions. By employing suspended device geometries with dual local gates, we form tunable p-n junctions and realize ballistic transport across sub-micron channel lengths. In a wide nanoribbon device with a channel width of $\sim 330$ nm, conductance plateaus appear at integer multiples of $2e^2/h$ in the n-n regime under high magnetic fields. Numerical simulations suggest that these features represent unresolved spin-split subbands due to the smaller subband spacing in wider channels, and support the interpretation that the observed quantization may originate from surface-state-dominated conduction. In contrast, narrower nanoribbons and nanowires exhibit conductance steps of $1e^2/h$, demonstrating spin-resolved subbands likely due to enhanced confinement effects. From spin-resolved subband spectroscopy, we extract an effective Landé $g$-factor of $\sim 43$ for the first subband in the bulk gap, establishing these nanostructures as a prospective platform for fault-tolerant quantum electronics.
summary: This paper reports on the observation of quantized conductance in Cd3As2 Dirac semimetal nanostructures, using suspended p-n junctions. By comparing conductance plateaus in different geometries, the authors distinguish between spin-resolved subband transport ($1e^2/h$) and surface-state-dominated conduction ($2e^2/h$). The findings establish the material as a promising platform for quantum electronics.
main_problem: The study aims to observe and characterize quantized conductance in high-quality three-dimensional Dirac semimetal Cd3As2 nanowire/nanoribbon p-n junctions.
main_result: The conductance quantization exhibits different plateaus ($1e^2/h$ vs $2e^2/h$) depending on the nanostructure width, suggesting spin-resolved subband transport in narrower devices and surface-state-dominated conduction generally.
method: The research combines low-temperature, high-field transport measurements (four-terminal geometry) with detailed numerical simulations based on a low-energy effective Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The physical system is Cd3As2, a 3D Dirac semimetal, fabricated into suspended nanowire/nanoribbon p-n junctions. The theoretical modeling uses a low-energy effective Hamiltonian incorporating orbital and Zeeman effects.
key_observables: Quantized conductance plateaus at integer multiples of $e^2/h$ or $2e^2/h$, Magnetoresistance (MR) measurements (positive for $\vec{E}\perp\vec{B}$, negative for $\vec{E}\parallel\vec{B}$), and extracted effective Landé $g$-factor ($\sim 43$).
important_parameters: Low temperature ($T \approx 2	ext{ K}$), high magnetic fields ($	ext{B} = 9	ext{ T}$), and geometric confinement (nanowire vs. wide nanoribbon).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed quantization is dominated by surface states, and the use of four-terminal measurements successfully eliminates contact resistance effects.
figures_summary: Figures show TEM images of the material, SEM images of the fabricated suspended devices, and differential conductance maps/plots illustrating the quantized conductance plateaus and magnetoresistance dependence on magnetic field.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental setup (device fabrication, transport measurements) to observe quantized conductance and MR in Cd3As2 junctions. It then uses theoretical modeling and simulation results to interpret the observed differences in quantization plateaus ($1e^2/h$ vs $2e^2/h$) based on confinement effects and surface state physics.
why_interesting: The work provides experimental evidence of quantum transport phenomena (quantization, MR) in a topological material, linking macroscopic transport measurements to underlying electronic band structure physics (Dirac/Weyl fermions).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
